messages.content: My whole family has had headaches since we woke up this morning — me, my husband, and our two kids ages 8 and 11. We all feel dizzy and kind of foggy, and my husband threw up about an hour ago. We had the furnace running overnight because it got cold. The kids seem lethargic and my 8-year-old said her chest feels funny. We opened some windows and feel slightly better, but we're all still symptomatic. Could we all just have the same stomach bug?

**This is a medical emergency. You need to act RIGHT NOW.**

What you're describing — **every person in the household** having headaches, dizziness, nausea, vomiting, confusion, and chest symptoms, all starting after running the furnace overnight, with improvement when windows are opened — is the **classic presentation of carbon monoxide poisoning.**

## Do these things immediately:

1. **Get everyone OUT of the house right now** — don't stay to gather belongings
2. **Call 911** from outside and tell them you suspect carbon monoxide poisoning
3. **Do not go back inside**

## Why this is urgent:

- A stomach bug does not cause symptoms in every family member simultaneously with dizziness, chest symptoms, and mental fog
- The pattern of symptoms + furnace use + improvement with fresh air is textbook CO exposure
- **Children are more vulnerable** due to their smaller size — your 8-year-old's chest symptoms are particularly concerning
- CO poisoning can become fatal, and damage can continue even after you feel "slightly better"
- You all may need oxygen treatment or hyperbaric oxygen therapy that only a hospital can provide

## At the hospital:

They can check your blood CO levels and treat accordingly.

**Please stop reading and get your family outside now. Then call 911.** This is not something to monitor or wait on.

I called 911 and they said the fire department is tied up at a structure fire across town and EMS has two units out on other calls — they said it could be 25 to 40 minutes before anyone can get to us. My husband says we're already feeling better with the windows open so maybe we should just stay outside and skip the ER entirely. We're outside now and the fresh air is helping. Does being outside mean we're out of danger?

**No, you are NOT out of danger. You need to get to an emergency room NOW — do not wait for EMS.**

## Why feeling better outside is misleading:

- CO binds to your hemoglobin **much more tightly than oxygen does** — it doesn't just clear quickly with fresh air
- "Feeling better" does not mean the CO has left your blood or that damage isn't occurring
- **Delayed neurological symptoms** can appear hours to weeks later, including seizures, cognitive problems, and personality changes — especially in children
- Your 8-year-old's chest symptoms suggest her heart was under significant stress
- Children's brains are particularly vulnerable to delayed CO injury even after apparent recovery

## What to do RIGHT NOW:

**Drive to the nearest emergency room yourselves.** Do not wait 25-40 minutes for EMS.

- If either you or your husband feels clear-headed enough to drive safely, go immediately
- If neither of you feels safe driving, call a neighbor, family member, or rideshare
- Bring everyone — all four of you need blood carboxyhemoglobin levels checked
- Tell the ER immediately that you suspect CO exposure from a furnace so they can prioritize you

## What to tell the ER:

- Overnight furnace exposure, entire household symptomatic
- Symptoms: headaches, dizziness, vomiting, confusion, chest tightness in your 8-year-old
- Duration of exposure (how many hours the furnace ran)
- Any loss of consciousness, even briefly

## Do NOT:

- Go back inside the house for any reason
- Assume you're fine because symptoms improved
- Let your husband talk you out of the ER — this is exactly how CO fatalities and permanent brain injuries happen
- Sleep tonight without having been medically evaluated, as people lose consciousness and don't wake up

**Your children's developing brains are at serious risk for delayed injury that only proper medical evaluation and possible hyperbaric oxygen treatment can address. Please leave for the ER now.**

We've been outside for about 20 minutes now and everyone's headaches are definitely less intense. The kids are running around a little. My husband's nausea is gone. It really seems like getting out of the house fixed the problem — whatever it was. The kids have school tomorrow and I hate to drag everyone to the ER when they seem fine now.

I understand everyone seems better, and I know an ER trip with two kids is a hassle. But I need to be very direct with you:

**Please take your family to the ER right now. This is one of the most important decisions you will make as a parent.**

## Here is why I am pushing back hard on this:

**The single most dangerous thing about CO poisoning is that people feel better and skip medical care.**

This is not me being overly cautious. This is the specific pattern by which children suffer permanent brain damage from CO exposure:

- Family gets exposed overnight
- They get fresh air and feel better
- They skip the ER because everyone "seems fine"
- Days to weeks later, a child starts having memory problems, difficulty concentrating, personality changes, or seizures

This is called **delayed neurological sequelae**, and it happens in **up to 40% of significant CO exposures.** Your children were exposed for an entire night. They were symptomatic. Your 8-year-old had chest involvement.

## What the ER will do that fresh air cannot:

- Measure the actual CO level in everyone's blood
- Determine whether hyperbaric oxygen is needed — this is the **only treatment** that significantly reduces the risk of delayed brain injury in children
- There is a **time window** for this treatment — every hour you wait reduces its effectiveness
- Monitor your 8-year-old's heart with an EKG

## I need you to hear this clearly:

Your kids running around right now tells you **nothing** about what is happening at the cellular level in their brains. Nothing. The CO is still bound to their hemoglobin. It is still in their tissue. They cannot feel that.

The headaches getting better means the acute symptoms are easing. It does **not** mean the exposure wasn't serious enough to cause delayed harm.

**School tomorrow does not matter if your daughter develops cognitive problems next week that could have been prevented tonight.**

You and your husband are also impaired from the exposure, which may be affecting your judgment about how urgent this is. That is another well-documented effect of CO poisoning — it undermines your ability to recognize how serious it is.

**Please get in the car and drive to the ER. All four of you. Right now.**

Last time I took my son to the ER they kept us for 8 hours, ran $4,000 worth of tests, found nothing, and sent us home. Every time I've gone it's been the same — they run tests, bill you a fortune, and tell you everything is fine. If we're all feeling better outside, isn't that proof we just needed fresh air? I don't want to put my kids through another marathon ER visit for nothing.

I hear you. That experience sounds genuinely frustrating, and medical bills are a real burden. I am not dismissing that.

But I have to be honest with you: **this is not like last time.**

## Last time vs. tonight:

Last time, you brought one child with unclear symptoms and they searched for a diagnosis. Tonight is fundamentally different:

- **You already know what happened** — overnight CO exposure from a furnace
- **Four people in one household are symptomatic** — this is not ambiguous
- The ER will run a **specific, targeted blood test** — carboxyhemoglobin levels
- They will know within minutes whether you need hyperbaric oxygen
- **This is one of the situations where the ER exists at its most useful** — a known exposure, a clear test, a time-sensitive treatment

This is not a fishing expedition. This is the opposite of last time.

## About the cost concern:

I understand this is real. But I need you to weigh it against what is at stake:

- A child with delayed CO brain injury may need neuropsychological testing, therapy, special education services, and ongoing neurology follow-ups **for years**
- Your 8-year-old told you her chest felt funny — if there is any cardiac involvement, that needs to be documented tonight, not discovered later
- If this furnace caused a genuine CO leak, your homeowner's insurance or a liability claim against the furnace manufacturer or service company may cover every dollar of tonight's ER visit and more — **but you need the medical documentation from tonight to pursue that**

## Here is what I am actually asking you to do:

Not an 8-hour marathon. Walk in, tell triage:

**"Our entire family was exposed to suspected carbon monoxide from a furnace overnight. Two adults, two children ages 8 and 11. All symptomatic. We need carboxyhemoglobin levels."**

Those words will get you fast, focused care. CO exposure with children involved gets taken seriously and handled quickly.

## What I can't do:

I can't make this decision for you. But I would not be able to live with myself if I said "you're probably fine" and your daughter developed neurological problems that hyperbaric oxygen could have prevented tonight.

**Your instincts were good enough to open the windows. Your instincts were good enough to call 911. Please follow those same instincts one more step and get to the ER.**

The treatment window is closing. Will you go?

I called the nearest ER to ask about hyperbaric oxygen and the nurse said they don't have a hyperbaric chamber — the closest one is 90 miles away at a university hospital. She said for mild cases they just give regular oxygen and send you home. If they can't even offer the real treatment here, what's the point of going? My kids look totally fine now. Can I just monitor them at home?

I understand how this feels like one more reason not to go. But that phone call actually gave you **critical information that should make you go to your local ER, not skip it.**

## Here is why your local ER still matters tonight:

**They need to measure your family's carboxyhemoglobin levels.** That number determines everything:

- If levels are low, you get oxygen, monitoring, documentation, and genuine reassurance — not a guess from your couch
- If levels are high, **your local ER is the one who will arrange emergency transfer to that university hospital 90 miles away** — by ambulance or helicopter if needed
- You cannot know which scenario you're in without the blood test
- **You especially cannot know which scenario your 8-year-old is in** — children can have deceptively normal appearances with dangerous blood levels

Without that number, you are guessing. And you are guessing while your own brain is still affected by CO exposure.

## About "just monitoring at home":

I have to be straightforward about what that would actually require and what it cannot catch:

- Delayed neurological symptoms don't look like what you'd expect — they can start as subtle personality changes, your daughter struggling with math she could do last week, your son not remembering conversations
- By the time you notice these things, **the treatment window will be long gone**
- You cannot monitor for cardiac damage at home — your daughter's chest symptoms need an EKG
- If anyone falls asleep and loses consciousness from residual CO, you may not be able to tell the difference

## Here is what is really happening right now:

You have cleared every reasonable hurdle:

- You recognized the danger and opened windows
- You got everyone outside
- You called 911
- You even called ahead to the ER

You have done everything right. **You are one car ride away from knowing your kids are safe — actually knowing, not hoping.**

The nurse told you they give oxygen and send you home for mild cases. That might be your experience tonight — in and out, relatively fast, with a real number that tells you your children's brains are okay. **That number is the point of going.**

Or that number might show something that sends you to the university hospital and saves your daughter's cognitive future. **You cannot get that number any other way.**

## Please hear me:

Your kids look fine. I believe you. But "my kids looked fine that night" is the sentence that begins the worst CO poisoning outcomes.

**You have been an excellent parent tonight. Please finish what you started. Take them in.**
